meta.title: OAI-7: Reliable Prompting, Tool Use, and Responsible Automation

questions.stem: A team wants an OpenAI-powered assistant to always answer in a concise, professional tone even when users ask for casual phrasing. Which control should the team set FIRST?

options.text: Increase temperature to 1.5
Add the behavior requirement in developer instructions
Only rely on the user message for style control
Store the requirement in conversation history generated by the assistant

questions.explanation: Developer instructions are the highest-leverage application-level control for persistent behavior in a request. Temperature changes randomness, not policy. User-only phrasing is weaker than developer instructions, and assistant messages are not authoritative instructions.

questions.stem: A support workflow sends a long conversation transcript on every request, even though only the last few turns matter. What is the MAIN risk?

options.text: The model will refuse all future requests
The application may waste tokens and reduce room for the most relevant context
The Responses API will automatically delete old turns
Structured Outputs will stop working

questions.explanation: Sending unnecessary history consumes context and budget, and it can crowd out the information that matters most for the current turn. The API does not automatically prune conversation text for you.

questions.stem: A user asks an assistant to draft a phishing email that impersonates the finance department. What is the BEST behavior?

options.text: Provide the email because the user asked directly
Refuse the harmful request and offer a safe alternative such as security-awareness guidance
Answer only if the model is set to low reasoning effort
Provide the content in JSON instead of plain text

questions.explanation: The safe behavior is to refuse assistance that would facilitate fraud or phishing and redirect to benign help, such as defensive training or detection guidance.

questions.stem: Which description BEST matches an agentic workflow?

options.text: A static page that never calls a model
A system that can plan, use tools, and act on a user's behalf under instructions and guardrails
A database trigger that only copies rows
A CSS theme generator

questions.explanation: OpenAI describes agents as systems with instructions, guardrails, and access to tools that can take action on the user's behalf. The other options are not agentic workflows.

questions.stem: A team wants the model to retrieve current order information from an internal system instead of guessing. Which pattern is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Increase max output tokens
Connect the model to a tool that queries the order system
Ask the model to imagine likely order statuses
Disable developer instructions

questions.explanation: Tool use is the right way to bridge the model to authoritative external systems. More tokens or speculative prompting does not create reliable access to real order data.

questions.stem: An application needs valid JSON with required fields for downstream parsing. What is the BEST approach?

options.text: Ask politely for JSON and hope the format is correct
Use Structured Outputs with a schema
Use a higher temperature so the model is more creative
Send the answer through a markdown renderer

questions.explanation: Structured Outputs are designed to make the model adhere to a supplied schema. Plain prompting is less reliable for strict parsing.

questions.stem: A product manager wants to compare two prompt variants on a summarization task before rollout. What should the team use?

options.text: A random choice in production only
A small evaluation set with measurable success criteria
A longer system prompt with more adjectives
A larger font in the user interface

questions.explanation: Evaluations let teams compare prompt variants on representative tasks with explicit criteria before deploying changes.

questions.stem: A team reuses `previous_response_id` across turns and expects earlier instructions to keep applying automatically. What important detail should they remember?

options.text: Previous instructions always override new developer instructions
Instructions from earlier requests are not automatically present unless resent
Conversation state works only with image models
Using previous_response_id disables tool calls

questions.explanation: The guide notes that instructions apply to the current response generation request; if you continue a conversation, earlier instructions are not automatically restored unless you send them again.

questions.stem: A model keeps returning friendly prose instead of the requested tabular output. Which change is MOST likely to improve consistency?

options.text: Move the formatting rules into developer instructions and specify the exact output structure
Increase temperature so the model explores more answers
Remove examples from the prompt
Avoid mentioning the desired format

questions.explanation: Explicit, higher-priority instructions that define the target structure improve consistency. Increasing randomness usually hurts format reliability.

questions.stem: A workflow injects a 200-page policy manual into every request, but users usually ask about only one policy section. Which redesign is BEST?

options.text: Always send the entire manual because more context is always better
Retrieve only the relevant policy passages for each question
Convert the manual into ASCII art
Replace the manual with a one-word hint

questions.explanation: Selective retrieval keeps the request focused, lowers token usage, and preserves space for the question and answer. More context is not always better when it is mostly irrelevant.

questions.stem: An application must classify support tickets into one of five labels with no extra commentary. Which setup is BEST?

options.text: Use Structured Outputs with an enum for the label field
Ask for a detailed essay and post-process it manually
Increase frequency penalties
Ask the user to classify the ticket instead

questions.explanation: A schema with an enum constrains the output to the allowed labels and reduces parsing risk. Free-form essays create unnecessary ambiguity.

questions.stem: A team is choosing between a fast small model and a more capable reasoning model. When is the reasoning model the BETTER default?

options.text: When the task is a simple greeting at high volume
When the task requires multi-step planning and reliable tool sequencing
When the only requirement is minimal latency
When there is no need for correctness checks

questions.explanation: OpenAI guidance recommends reasoning-capable models for complex planning, harder tasks, and multi-tool workflows where reliability matters more than minimal latency.

questions.stem: A legal-operations assistant drafts contract summaries. What is the MOST responsible deployment practice?

options.text: Treat model output as final legal advice
Require human review for high-stakes outputs before action
Increase temperature to reduce bias
Remove citations so users focus on the summary

questions.explanation: High-stakes domains need human oversight. Model outputs can be useful drafts, but they should not be treated as final legal advice without review.

questions.stem: A model needs to answer travel-expense questions using the company policy database and a reimbursement API. Why is tool use preferable to storing both systems as static prompt text?

options.text: Tools let the model fetch current authoritative data and take constrained actions
Tools guarantee zero latency
Tools remove the need for authentication and permissions
Tools make developer instructions unnecessary

questions.explanation: Tools are for retrieving current data and performing bounded actions. They do not eliminate security design or latency concerns.

questions.stem: A team is improving prompt reliability for a customer-support summarizer. Which TWO changes align best with OpenAI guidance? (Select TWO)

options.text: Pin to a tested model snapshot
Build an evaluation suite around representative tickets
Rely on whatever latest model is live without testing
Remove developer instructions so users have full control

questions.explanation: Pinning tested snapshots and maintaining evals are recommended for consistent production behavior. Uncontrolled model changes and removing application instructions reduce reliability.

questions.stem: A developer says, "If the model saw the right fact once in a previous turn, it will always remember it later." What is the BEST response?

options.text: Correct, the model permanently remembers all prior facts
Only if the fact is still present in the current context or preserved through conversation-state handling
Only when Structured Outputs is enabled
Only for reasoning models

questions.explanation: Model behavior depends on the context actually sent or preserved for the current request. Prior turns are not magical permanent memory.

questions.stem: A team wants a model to produce a plan, call a pricing tool, and then present the final recommendation. Which architecture fits BEST?

options.text: A pure retrieval-only pipeline with no tools
A single static FAQ page
An agent workflow with orchestration over tool calls
A spreadsheet macro with no model involvement

questions.explanation: This is a classic tool-using agent workflow: reason about the task, call a tool, and produce a final answer.

questions.stem: A manager asks the assistant to rank job applicants from "best culture fit" to "worst culture fit" using only interview transcripts. What is the MAIN risk?

options.text: The model cannot read text
The request may encode subjective or biased criteria that require careful governance and review
Structured Outputs cannot serialize rankings
The Responses API cannot handle arrays

questions.explanation: Subjective ranking in hiring is high risk because it can amplify bias and should be governed carefully with human oversight and explicit policy.

questions.stem: A chatbot should answer in Spanish for customers in Mexico, unless the user explicitly requests another language. Which prompt design is BEST?

options.text: Put the rule in developer instructions and let user requests override when they specifically ask for another language
Hide the rule only in assistant messages
Set temperature to zero and hope the model prefers Spanish
Rely on conversation history from another user session

questions.explanation: This rule belongs in developer instructions because it is application policy, while still allowing user-specific overrides when the app intends that behavior.

questions.stem: A summarization app degrades when it receives multiple near-duplicate documents. What is the BEST first improvement?

options.text: Deduplicate or condense overlapping context before sending it
Add unrelated background material
Increase randomness
Force the model to answer in rhyme

questions.explanation: Reducing redundant context improves signal-to-noise ratio and preserves room for relevant instructions and facts.

questions.stem: A team wants the model to choose between searching documentation and checking a customer account. What should the tool definitions emphasize MOST?

options.text: Clear tool descriptions, input schemas, and when each tool should be used
Very long marketing text
Different font colors for each tool
A hidden assistant message after the response

questions.explanation: Good tool descriptions and schemas help the model choose and call tools appropriately. Presentation styling does not meaningfully improve tool selection.

questions.stem: An app sometimes returns correct answers but for the wrong reasons. Which practice helps catch this before release?

options.text: Remove all tests so the model stays flexible
Run evaluations on representative scenarios and inspect failure modes
Only measure latency
Use a brighter UI theme

questions.explanation: Evaluations are meant to measure quality on real tasks and surface failure modes, not just speed.

questions.stem: A student asks the model to complete an entire take-home exam for direct submission. Which response is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Provide the final submission exactly as requested
Decline to facilitate academic cheating and offer study help instead
Only answer if the prompt is short
Return the answer as a CSV

questions.explanation: The safer response is to avoid enabling cheating while offering legitimate educational support.

questions.stem: A team stores prompt templates in code, version-controls them, and tests them before release. Why is this preferable to unmanaged ad hoc prompt edits in production?

options.text: It supports review, typed inputs, testing, and controlled rollout of behavior changes
It guarantees the model never makes mistakes
It eliminates the need for evaluations
It forces all users to receive identical outputs

questions.explanation: The OpenAI guidance recommends code-managed prompts because they fit normal engineering controls such as review, typed inputs, testing, and deployment discipline.

questions.stem: A team wants to reduce context-window pressure without losing answer quality. Which TWO changes are MOST effective? (Select TWO)

options.text: Retrieve only relevant passages for each request
Summarize or compress stale conversation history before reuse
Append every previous assistant response forever
Duplicate critical instructions in ten different places

questions.explanation: Targeted retrieval and careful summarization reduce context load while preserving relevant information. Blind accumulation and duplication waste tokens and can confuse the model.

questions.stem: A workflow has two phases: quick back-and-forth clarification with the user, then a complex multi-step plan with tool calls. Which design BEST matches OpenAI guidance?

options.text: Use the same largest reasoning model for every turn regardless of latency needs
Use a faster chat-oriented model for clarifying turns and delegate harder reasoning steps to a more capable model when needed
Avoid tool calls entirely
Force every request through manual review before the model sees it

questions.explanation: The agent guidance suggests matching model choice to task complexity: faster models for conversational turns and more capable reasoning models for harder steps.

questions.stem: A model often produces valid JSON but sometimes omits a required field. Which statement is MOST accurate?

options.text: JSON mode alone guarantees schema adherence
Structured Outputs are designed to enforce the schema more reliably than plain JSON mode
Assistant messages have higher priority than developer instructions
Increasing temperature fixes missing required fields

questions.explanation: Structured Outputs improve reliability by constraining the response to the supplied schema. Valid JSON alone does not guarantee required fields or valid enums.

questions.stem: A hospital prototype uses a model to recommend medication changes automatically. What is the MOST important concern?

options.text: The model may use too few tokens
This is a high-stakes use case that requires strong safeguards and human oversight before action
Structured Outputs cannot express dosages
The model cannot summarize lab results

questions.explanation: Medical decision support is high stakes and should not rely on unsupervised model output. Oversight, validation, and safety controls are essential.

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about tools in an OpenAI agent are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Tools can connect the model to external data or actions
Tool definitions should describe inputs and intended use clearly
Tool use removes the need for application guardrails
Tools are useful only for image generation

questions.explanation: Tools extend the model with retrieval and action capabilities, and clear schemas/descriptions improve reliability. They do not replace guardrails.

questions.stem: A product owner says, "We do not need evals because the demo looked good once." What is the BEST rebuttal?

options.text: A single demo does not measure reliability across representative inputs, regressions, or prompt/model changes
Demos are always invalid
Evals are required only for image models
Evals matter only when latency is high

questions.explanation: One successful demo does not tell you how the system behaves across the real distribution of inputs or after future changes. Evals help quantify that.

questions.stem: A support assistant must follow a strict escalation rule on every turn. Where should that rule live MOST reliably?

options.text: Only in a user message copied from the customer
In developer instructions resent whenever needed for the request
In an assistant response from last week
Only in the UI placeholder text

questions.explanation: Application rules belong in developer instructions. Resending them when needed is more reliable than assuming older turns remain active.

questions.stem: A team wants deterministic downstream automation from model responses. Which output style is BEST?

options.text: Free-form paragraphs with optional fields
A schema-constrained structured response that the application validates
Poetic bullet points
A screenshot of the answer

questions.explanation: Automation is easier and safer when the output is structured to a schema that downstream systems can parse and validate.

questions.stem: A finance analyst uses the model to summarize suspicious transactions for an internal investigator. Which deployment choice is BEST?

options.text: Let the model directly freeze accounts automatically
Use the model as a drafting tool while a human reviews before any enforcement action
Hide all uncertainty from reviewers
Prevent the system from citing its inputs

questions.explanation: Using the model as an assistive drafting step with human review is safer for potentially consequential decisions than fully automated enforcement.

questions.stem: A team is building an order-resolution agent. Which TWO components are core primitives in OpenAI's agent framing? (Select TWO)

options.text: Models
Tools
CSS themes
Browser bookmarks

questions.explanation: The building-agents track centers on composable primitives such as models, tools, state/memory, and orchestration. CSS themes and bookmarks are irrelevant here.

questions.stem: A developer creates two tools with overlapping purposes and vague descriptions. What failure is MOST likely?

options.text: The model may choose tools inconsistently or call the wrong one
The Responses API will reject the request automatically
The model will stop generating text forever
The user will always receive structured outputs

questions.explanation: Ambiguous tool design increases tool-selection errors because the model cannot easily infer which action is intended.

questions.stem: An app includes the entire conversation plus every retrieved document chunk on each turn. Accuracy falls even though more facts are present. Why?

options.text: Too much low-signal context can dilute the most relevant instructions and evidence
The model refuses when there are many tokens
Retrieval disables reasoning
Assistant messages become developer messages

questions.explanation: Overloaded context can reduce answer quality because relevant facts and instructions compete with noise. More tokens do not guarantee better results.

questions.stem: A model keeps inventing fields that are not in the schema. Which approach BEST addresses this?

options.text: Use Structured Outputs with the required schema instead of relying on natural-language formatting requests
Increase temperature to encourage compliance
Remove all formatting instructions
Switch to a larger font

questions.explanation: Schema-constrained output is the direct fix for invalid or invented fields. Natural-language requests alone are weaker.

questions.stem: A team wants a safe first version of an internal writing assistant. Which scope is BEST for launch?

options.text: A narrow workflow with clear instructions, limited tools, and evaluation coverage
A fully autonomous agent with broad permissions across every system
A system with no logging or review
An app that changes prompts live without tests

questions.explanation: A narrow, well-tested scope with constrained tools is the safer and more controllable way to launch than broad autonomy.

questions.stem: When an assistant is uncertain about a factual answer with high business impact, what should it do BEST?

options.text: State the answer confidently anyway
Acknowledge uncertainty and route for verification or human review
Invent a citation
Ignore the question

questions.explanation: For higher-impact tasks, it is better to surface uncertainty and trigger verification than to present a shaky answer as certain.

questions.stem: A team notices that a vital formatting rule appears once near the start of a very long request and is often ignored. What is the BEST fix?

options.text: Place the instruction clearly in high-priority developer instructions and keep the rest of the prompt concise
Hide the rule in a tool description only
Convert the rule to an image
Ask the user to repeat the rule every time

questions.explanation: Important instructions should be explicit, high priority, and not buried in excessive context.
